title: St Rumbold's Cathedral

infobox.value: ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Sint-Romboutskathedraal*; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Cathédrale Saint-Rombaut*) is the [Roman Catholic metropolitan archiepiscopal](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Mechelen-Brussels "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Mechelen-Brussels") [cathedral](/wiki/Cathedral "Cathedral") in [Mechelen](/wiki/Mechelen "Mechelen"), Belgium, dedicated to [Saint Rumbold](/wiki/Rumbold_of_Mechlin "Rumbold of Mechlin"), Christian [missionary](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") and [martyr](/wiki/Martyr "Martyr") who founded an [abbey](/wiki/Abbey#Benedictine_monasteries "Abbey") nearby.[[1]](#cite_note-DE-HRum-1)[[2]](#cite_note-DvdA-Fred-2)[[3]](#cite_note-CathOL-3)[[4]](#cite_note-SRAbbeyToCathedral-source-4) His remains are rumoured to be buried inside the cathedral. State-of-the-art examination of the relics honoured as Saint Rumbold's and kept in a shrine in the [retro-choir](/wiki/Retro-choir "Retro-choir"), showed a life span of about 40 years and a death date between 580 and 655, while tradition had claimed 775 AD.[[Note 1]](#cite_note-SRAbbey-M-5)[[5]](#cite_note-tradition-6)[[6]](#cite_note-2004date1-7)[[4]](#cite_note-SRAbbeyToCathedral-source-4)[[7]](#cite_note-2004date2-8)

Cathedral in Mechelen, Belgium

**St. Rumbold's Cathedral** ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Sint-Romboutskathedraal*; [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Cathédrale Saint-Rombaut*) is the [Roman Catholic metropolitan archiepiscopal](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Mechelen-Brussels "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Mechelen-Brussels") [cathedral](/wiki/Cathedral "Cathedral") in [Mechelen](/wiki/Mechelen "Mechelen"), Belgium, dedicated to [Saint Rumbold](/wiki/Rumbold_of_Mechlin "Rumbold of Mechlin"), Christian [missionary](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") and [martyr](/wiki/Martyr "Martyr") who founded an [abbey](/wiki/Abbey#Benedictine_monasteries "Abbey") nearby.[[1]](#cite_note-DE-HRum-1)[[2]](#cite_note-DvdA-Fred-2)[[3]](#cite_note-CathOL-3)[[4]](#cite_note-SRAbbeyToCathedral-source-4) His remains are rumoured to be buried inside the cathedral. State-of-the-art examination of the relics honoured as Saint Rumbold's and kept in a shrine in the [retro-choir](/wiki/Retro-choir "Retro-choir"), showed a life span of about 40 years and a death date between 580 and 655, while tradition had claimed 775 AD.[[Note 1]](#cite_note-SRAbbey-M-5)[[5]](#cite_note-tradition-6)[[6]](#cite_note-2004date1-7)[[4]](#cite_note-SRAbbeyToCathedral-source-4)[[7]](#cite_note-2004date2-8)

In 1999, the tower of the cathedral was inscribed on the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") [World Heritage List](/wiki/World_Heritage_List "World Heritage List") as part of the [Belfries of Belgium and France](/wiki/Belfries_of_Belgium_and_France "Belfries of Belgium and France") site, in recognition of its architecture and its importance in civic duties such as a watchtower.[[8]](#cite_note-unesco-9)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Rumbold%27s_Cathedral&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Construction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Rumbold%27s_Cathedral&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Construction")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Wenceslas_Hollar_-_The_tower_at_Malines.jpg/120px-Wenceslas_Hollar_-_The_tower_at_Malines.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wenceslas_Hollar_-_The_tower_at_Malines.jpg)

A 167 m finished tower design engraved in 1649 by [Wenceslaus Hollar](/wiki/Wenceslaus_Hollar "Wenceslaus Hollar"), with the headline: TVRRIS ELEGANTISSIMÆ S. RVMOLDI MECHLINIÆ

Construction of the church itself started shortly after 1200, and it was [consecrated](/wiki/Consecration "Consecration") in 1312, when part had become usable. From 1324 onwards the [flying buttresses](/wiki/Flying_buttress "Flying buttress") and revised [choir](/wiki/Choir_(architecture) "Choir (architecture)") structure acquired [Brabantine Gothic](/wiki/Brabantine_Gothic "Brabantine Gothic") characteristics, distinct from [French Gothic](/wiki/French_Gothic "French Gothic"). After the city fire of 1342, the [Master Mason](/wiki/Master_Mason "Master Mason") [Jean d'Oisy](/wiki/Jean_d%27Oisy "Jean d'Oisy") managed repairs and continued this second phase, which by the time of his death in 1375 formed the prototype for that High Gothic style. His successors finished the [vaults](/wiki/Vault_(architecture) "Vault (architecture)") of the [nave](/wiki/Nave "Nave") by 1437, and those of the choir by 1451.[[4]](#cite_note-SRAbbeyToCathedral-source-4)[[9]](#cite_note-SRk-Lier-10)[[10]](#cite_note-stvt-11)[[11]](#cite_note-SRT-A-12)

During the final phase of 1452–1520, the [tower](/wiki/Tower "Tower") was erected, financed by pilgrims and later by its proprietor, the city. Designed to reach 600 Mechlinian feet[[Note 2]](#cite_note-Tower-design-13) or about 167 metres, higher than any church tower would ever attain ([Ulm Minster](/wiki/Ulm_Minster "Ulm Minster") has measured 161 metres since the 19th century), the very heavy St. Rumbold's tower was built on what had once been wetlands, though with foundations only three metres deep its site appears to have been well-chosen. After a few years, in 1454, its chief architect [Andries I Keldermans](/wiki/Keldermans_family "Keldermans family") constructed the [Saint Livinus' Monster Tower](/wiki/Sint-Lievensmonstertoren "Sint-Lievensmonstertoren") (or *St.-Lievensmonstertoren* as it is called in Dutch) in [Zierikzee](/wiki/Zierikzee "Zierikzee") (in the present-day Netherlands), where leaning or sagging of the tower (now 62 metres but designed for ca. 130) could wreck the church. This concern led to fully separate edifices, a solution also applied in Mechelen. At both places, in the early 16th century the upper part of the tower was abandoned, not for technical but for financial reasons. St-Rumbold's should have been topped by a 77-metre [spire](/wiki/Spire "Spire") but only seven metres of this were built, hence the unusual shape. A deliberately weak connection closed the gap between tower and church upon finishing the construction.[[12]](#cite_note-SRT-H-e-14)[[13]](#cite_note-Monster1-15)[[14]](#cite_note-Monster2-16)[[15]](#cite_note-DTsoK-17)

### Later history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Rumbold%27s_Cathedral&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Later history")]

The church has functioned as a cathedral since 1559. In the 18th century, each [capital](/wiki/Capital_(architecture) "Capital (architecture)")'s surrounding ornament of sculpted cabbage leaves, that had been an inspiration for numerous Brabantine Gothic churches, was replaced with a double ring of crops. In 2005, after engineers had figured out the [support capacity](/wiki/Bearing_capacity "Bearing capacity") of ground and tower, there was talk of completing the entire spire from the original drawings.[[16]](#cite_note-Ehk-18) In 2010, prior to the construction of an underground car park at Saint Rumbold's north side, 4,165 skeletons were unearthed during archeological excavations of the cemetery.[[17]](#cite_note-19)

## Tower

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Rumbold%27s_Cathedral&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Tower")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/SkywalkSintRombouts.jpg/250px-SkywalkSintRombouts.jpg)](/wiki/File:SkywalkSintRombouts.jpg)

Unhindered view of the distant horizon (up to [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") and [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels")) is possible since 2009 from the Skywalk.

The flat-topped [silhouette](/wiki/Silhouette "Silhouette") of the cathedral's tower is easily recognizable and dominates the surroundings. For centuries it held the city documents, served as a [watchtower](/wiki/Watchtower "Watchtower"), and could sound the fire alarm.[[18]](#cite_note-TM!-20)
Despite its characteristic incompleteness, this [World Heritage monument](/wiki/World_Heritage_Sites "World Heritage Sites")[[19]](#cite_note-IL_WH-21)
is 97.28 metres high and its 514 steps are mounted by thousands of [tourists](/wiki/Tourist "Tourist") every year, following the footsteps of [Louis XV](/wiki/Louis_XV "Louis XV"), [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon_Bonaparte "Napoleon Bonaparte"), [King Albert I](/wiki/Albert_I_of_Belgium "Albert I of Belgium"), and [King Baudouin](/wiki/Baudouin_of_Belgium "Baudouin of Belgium") with [queen Fabiola](/wiki/Queen_Fabiola_of_Belgium "Queen Fabiola of Belgium") in 1981.

Many of the region's cities have a nickname for their [populace](/wiki/Populace "Populace"). The Mechlinians are said to have had ancestors running up their great Tower and passing on buckets of water to extinguish a blazing fire behind the perpendicular windows, where it turned out to be mere [moonlight](/wiki/Moonlight "Moonlight") through sprightly clouds, hence are called *Maneblussers* ('Moon Extinguishers').[[20]](#cite_note-SRT-Tw-22)[[Note 3]](#cite_note-Fire-1687-23)

## Bells

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Rumbold%27s_Cathedral&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Bells")]

The tower contains 49 [bells](/wiki/Bell_(instrument) "Bell (instrument)") and only the 6 largest bells swing, which are still in working order.[[21]](#cite_note-24) The weights of the bells range from 16 kg to the most notable bell; the bourdon *Salvator*, which strikes the hours and weighs 8 tons. Up to 1923, the cathedral had 18 bell ringers prior to electricity taking over. Thirty-nine steps above this instrument, there is a second complete [carillon](/wiki/Carillon "Carillon") on which concerts are played during the summer months.

## Interior

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Rumbold%27s_Cathedral&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Interior")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Mechelen_Sint-Rombouts.JPG/250px-Mechelen_Sint-Rombouts.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mechelen_Sint-Rombouts.JPG)

St. Rumbold's Cathedral towering above the *Grote Markt* square

The main entrance, underneath the tower, leads into the nave of the cathedral (approximately 118 metres long).

Apart from small heraldic shields,[[Note 4]](#cite_note-TheHague-25) dating from the [Thirty Knights of the Golden Fleece](/wiki/Order_of_the_Golden_Fleece "Order of the Golden Fleece") chapter meetings presided in the church by young [Philip the Handsome](/wiki/Philip_I_of_Castile "Philip I of Castile") while his [Burgundian inheritance](/wiki/Mary_of_Burgundy#The_Burgundian_inheritance_and_the_Spanish_alliance "Mary of Burgundy") was still under guardianship of [his father](/wiki/Maximilian_I,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Maximilian I, Holy Roman Emperor"), few original movables survive. Forty preciously decorated Gothic altars and all other furniture disappeared during the [religious troubles of 1566–1585](/wiki/Eighty_Years%27_War "Eighty Years' War"). Though the cathedral was spared in the 1566 [Iconoclasm](/wiki/Iconoclasm "Iconoclasm"), Mechelen was sacked in the 1572 three-days *[Spanish Fury](/wiki/Spanish_Fury "Spanish Fury")* by slaughtering troops under command of [Alva](/wiki/Fernando_%C3%81lvarez_de_Toledo,_3rd_Duke_of_Alba "Fernando Álvarez de Toledo, 3rd Duke of Alba")'s son [Fadrique](/wiki/Fadrique_%C3%81lvarez_de_Toledo,_4th_Duke_of_Alba "Fadrique Álvarez de Toledo, 4th Duke of Alba"), and suffered the [English Fury](/wiki/English_Fury_at_Mechelen "English Fury at Mechelen") of pillaging by rampant mercenaries in the service of the [States General](/wiki/States_General_of_the_Netherlands "States General of the Netherlands") in 1580.

The interior features a [Baroque](/wiki/Baroque "Baroque") high altar and [choir](/wiki/Choir_(architecture) "Choir (architecture)") by [Lucas Faydherbe](/wiki/Lucas_Faydherbe "Lucas Faydherbe") (with twenty-five paintings illustrating the life of Saint Rumbold), as well as paintings by [Anthony van Dyck](/wiki/Anthony_van_Dyck "Anthony van Dyck"), sculptures by [Lucas Faydherbe](/wiki/Lucas_Faydherbe "Lucas Faydherbe") and Michiel Vervoort, and [stained-glass](/wiki/Cathedral_glass "Cathedral glass") windows,[[22]](#cite_note-IL_SW-ph-26)
including one depicting – though with a white face – the [Black Madonna](/wiki/Black_Madonna "Black Madonna") painting in the church.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*][[Note 5]](#cite_note-BMad-27)

## Organs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Rumbold%27s_Cathedral&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Organs")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/St_Rombout_Organ_2.jpg/250px-St_Rombout_Organ_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Rombout_Organ_2.jpg)

The Gallery Organ, built by Stevens

The cathedral has two organs. The main organ on the reverse side of the west facade was built in 1957 by organ builder Stevens. On the reverse of the side facade is a smaller organ, installed there in 1919 by the same organ builder Stevens. This instrument has 30 stops on two manual keyboards and pedalboard.

## Events at the cathedral

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Rumbold%27s_Cathedral&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Events at the cathedral")]

St. Rumbold's was the venue for the 2008 wedding of [Count Rodolphe de Limburg Stirum](/wiki/House_of_Limburg-Stirum "House of Limburg-Stirum") to [Archduchess Marie-Christine of Austria](/wiki/Archduke_Carl_Christian_of_Austria "Archduke Carl Christian of Austria"), daughter of [Princess Marie-Astrid of Luxembourg](/wiki/Archduchess_Marie_Astrid_of_Austria "Archduchess Marie Astrid of Austria").

### Papal visit

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Rumbold%27s_Cathedral&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Papal visit")]

In 1985, on his 65th birthday, [Pope John Paul II](/wiki/Pope_John_Paul_II "Pope John Paul II") celebrated a [Mass](/wiki/Mass_(liturgy) "Mass (liturgy)") at St. Rumbold's. Jo Haazen, then the city's [carillon](/wiki/Carillon "Carillon") player, heard him state: "Your tower is not complete."[[16]](#cite_note-Ehk-18)[[23]](#cite_note-SRK-PM-28)[[Note 6]](#cite_note-PJPII_1-29)

## Notes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=St._Rumbold%27s_Cathedral&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Notes")]

1. **[^](#cite_ref-SRAbbey-M_5-0)** The abbey founded by St. Rumbold in the 6th, 7th or 8th century and a 9th century St. Rumbold's abbey church subordinate to the [bishops of Liège](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Prince-Bishopric of Liège") are assumed to have been located in the *Holm*, higher grounds a little outside the later city walls of Mechelen. A 9th century St. Rumbold's Chapel in the city centre stood till 1580, was rebuilt in 1597 and demolished in 1798. After [Prince-Bishop Notger](/wiki/Notker_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Notker of Liège")'s founding of the St. Rumbold's [Chapter](/wiki/Chapter_(religion) "Chapter (religion)") around 1000, an adjacent collegiate church was built and its parish title was handed to the chapter in 1134. Most likely on its spot, already from around the next turn of the century onwards the well known St. Rumbold's Church was built, consecrated in 1312, and promoted to cathedral in 1559. This edifice never belonged to the abbey.
2. **[^](#cite_ref-Tower-design_13-0)** The original designer of St. Rumbold's tower may have been Jan II Keldermans, Andries I Keldermans, or Wouter Coolman. [(Source retrieved 25 July 2011)](http://sintromboutstoren.mechelen.be/nl/3609/content/9139/torenweetjes.html) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20180318154302/http://sintromboutstoren.mechelen.be/nl/3609/content/9139/torenweetjes.html) 18 March 2018 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine"). The now obsolete local foot came to 27.8 centimetres, roughly an inch shorter than the 30.48-cm long current British and American measure.
3. **[^](#cite_ref-Fire-1687_23-0)** The full moon of 27 January 1687 caused all the stir about the presumed tower fire, reported as a 'happy-ending tragedy'. According to an 1808 sale's list Servais, Gaspar Joseph de (1808). ["item 4242"](https://books.google.com/books?id=dFAVAAAAQAAJ&pg=PA310). *Catalogue des livres de la bibliothèque de feu monsieur Gaspar-Joseph de Servais (in French)*. Retrieved 28 July 2011. "4242. Maan – suchtigen Brand – sticht of Mechelsche beroerte. Bly-eyndend Treur-spel op St.-Rombouts Tooren den 27 january 1687, in volle maen, in-8°. met platen. V." (in outdated Dutch)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-TheHague_25-0)** The small painted shields at St. Rumbold's should not be confused with the armour remnants in the Large Church of [The Hague](/wiki/The_Hague "The Hague") where (as in the Knights Hall there) the earliest Thirty Knights of the Golden Fleece had convened in 1456. ([Source](http://www.denhaag.nl/en/business/international-The-Hague/to/A-Short-History-of-The-Hague.htm) [Archived](https://web.archive.org/web/20110928144402/http://www.denhaag.nl/en/business/international-The-Hague/to/A-Short-History-of-The-Hague.htm) 28 September 2011 at the [Wayback Machine](/wiki/Wayback_Machine "Wayback Machine") retrieved 31 July 2011)
5. **[^](#cite_ref-BMad_27-0)** The texts above and below the Madonna in the [stained glass](/wiki/Stained_glass_window "Stained glass window") indicate portraying the Black Madonna painting in the cathedral; details of painting and its frame decoration however, are dissimilar.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-PJPII_1_29-0)** The [papal](/wiki/Pope "Pope") visit of Mechelen in 1985 included also the [Church of Our Lady of Hanswijk](/wiki/Basilica_of_Our_Lady_of_Hanswijk "Basilica of Our Lady of Hanswijk"), which John Paul II granted the title of basilica in 1987.
